title: 2026/01/29 - FD (Part 1)
displayTitle: 2026-01-29 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - Briefing on meetings with representative counterparts and government officials [Part 1]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: Briefing on meetings with representative counterparts and government officials
description: Thursday 29 January 2026 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Briefing on meetings with representative counterparts and government officials Hearing of evidence: List of submitters: Delegation from Kingdom of Morocco Her Excellency Mrs Wassane Zailachi, Ambassador International Treaty Examination of the Agreement to Establish the Pacific Resilience Facility Hearing of evidence: List of submitters: Council for International Development Aotearoa New Zealand Todd Cleaver, Executive Director Sam London, Standards and Humanitarian Manager Peace Movement Aotearoa Edwina Hughes, Coordinator PILLAR NZ Arian Tashakkori, Head of Operations and Donor Relations Nathan Seuili, Executive Director
descriptionSummary: Briefing on meetings with representative counterparts and government officials. List of. Delegation from Kingdom of Morocco

chunks.text: Good morning. Good morning. Nice to see you again.

Alright, well welcome along to the Foreign Affairs Defense and Trade Committee, your excellency, great to have you here in New Zealand with us. Uh, we are live streaming this session as we do with our public hearings, and we have half an hour sit down for this morning's, uh, discussion.

We've received a hard copy of your presentation. Thank you for sending that through. Wonderful. And we would welcome your comments to speak to that. And then I'm sure there'll be questions from committee members as well. I Hope so. Thank you. Over To you. Well, first of all, thank you very much for having me.

Thank you for taking the time and, uh, I had the pleasure and honor of, uh, meeting you and when you are in Canberra, so I look forward to, uh, our discussion. Thank you. So it's, it's a great honor to appear before this committee, and I sincerely thank you for the opportunity to engage in a dialogue with New Zealand's Parliament's parliament on Morocco's vision, our regional role, and the prospects for deeper cooperation between our two countries.

Although geographically distant Morocco and New Zealand share essential values, respect for international law, commitment to multilateralism, openness to trade, and a deep belief in sustainable development. These shared principles form a strong foundation for partnership.

Morocco has always considered New Zealand a longstanding friend, yet despite this goodwill, our bilateral relations, whether political, parliamentary, or economic, deserve to be strengthened through regular exchanges and structured strategic dialogue. I fully understand the tyray of distance geo geography in inevitably shapes perceptions, but distance should not prevent engagement, especially when Morocco plays an active role in areas that matter to New Zealand.

Counter-terrorism, energy transition, food security, and regional stability. There is a clear political will in Morocco to deepen our relationship with New Zealand. Today, I wish to demonstrate why Morocco can be a strategic partner for your country and why the time has come to elevate our relationship.

So why Morocco? Morocco is a politically stable country anchored in continuous reform. Our 2011 con constitution expanded rights to strengthen decentralization and consolidated democratic governance. It it reflects a long-term vision based on stability, inclusion, and institutional resilience.

Our national identity is plural and inclusive it in it, it integrates Arab Amazi, Saharan African, and delusion Jewish and Mediterranean components. Morocco is the only Arab country to have a Jewish museum, and we officially recognize the Shoah.

One of his Majesty's closest advisors, Mr. Andre Azule, is Jewish. Diversity in Morocco is not symbolic. It is aru, it is structural and lived in a fragmented and volatile region. Morocco acts as a stability anchor and a bridge between north and south Europe and Africa, the Atlantic, and the Mediterranean.

We are also active. We are also an active counter-terrorism partner. Robot hosts the United Nations office for counterterrorism, where Australia is already engaged. Morocco's approach is holistic, combining security prevention, religious moderat, re moderation, rehabilitation and integration, particularly through the Mohammedi Institute for the training of Imams, Modi, and Morida, and the Muha program, which means reconciliation.

Morocco itself was a victim of terrorism in 2003 and 2011. Tragic events such as Christchurch or Bond Bondi remind us that terrorism is global distance does not shield any nation. Morocco's experience in promoting moderate Islam and co combating extreme can be of concrete relevance to New Zealand.

Over the past two decades, Morocco has undergone a profound economic transformation. We have built a diversified industrial base in automotive, manufacturing, aerospace, agri industry, and advanced logistics. Morocco's now a regional manufacturing and export hub connecting Europe, Africa, and the Atlantic.

This transformation is underpinned by world class infrastructure. The port of Tan Med is today the leading port in the Mediterranean and in Africa, ranking among the top global hubs for container traffic and logistics performance. 18. It anchors industrial ecosystems in automotive and aerospace manufacturing and connects Morocco to more than 180 ports worldwide.

In the south. The development of DLA embodies forward-looking vision. DLA is emerging as a strategic Atlantic gateway, combining logistics, fisheries, renewable energy and agribusiness in positioning the region in a bridge between North and West Africa and the wider Atlantic space.

Morocco's attractiveness is also driven by its trade a, uh, architecture. The kingdom has concluded preferential trade agreements with 62 countries, including the European Union, the United States, Turkey, and many African and Arab partners. This network offers companies based in Morocco privilege access to a market of over 1 billion consumers making Morocco a highly competitive export platform in energy.

Morocco is a pioneer. The Nor solar complex is one of the largest in the world. We are Africa's leader in wind energy and have a set ambitious target, 52% of electricity by 2030. Right now we are at 47%. Morocco is also positioning itself as if future green hy hydrogen hub, leveraging its solar and wind potential, its ports and its proximity to major markets and area of di direct strategic relevance For New Zealand's own energy transition and climate objectives.

Morocco is deeply anchored in Africa. We are the first investors in West Africa, and the second across the continent with strong and growing f footprints in banking, telecommunications, energy, and agriculture. With more than 100 companies, Moroccan banks, telecom operators, energy companies and agribusiness firms are now present throughout Africa.

OCP are, are, um, company for phosphates, uh, operates in 12 countries playing a central role in food security and sustainable agriculture. Morocco, uh, uh, detaining more than 70% of world reserves in phosphates. A flagship project illustrating this vision is the Nigeria Morocco gas pipeline.

Spanning 13 countries will serve over 400 million people enable industrialization and foster regional integration across West Africa and beyond. These initiatives are part of a broader vision for Africa and the Atlantic space. His majesty, the king, has launched strategic initiatives aimed at transforming the Atlantic facade of Africa into a new quarter of development, connectivity, and shared prosperity.

This Atlantic initiative seeks to give Landlock Sahil countries access to the ocean, facilitate trade routes, and has energy and digital connectivity, and promote development across West Africa through these royal initiatives. Morocco is positioning itself, not merely as an African actor, but as an Atlantic gateway linking Africa with Europe and the Americas, and offering partners a stable, predictable and forward looking platform for engagement with political stability and a legal economic framework.

Modern infrastructure Morocco stands in as an ideal platform for Africa bound investment for partners seeking a strategic entry point into a rapidly transforming continent. Morocco and New Zealand established diplomatic relations in 1976. This being our 50th year, we share a strong attachment to multilateralism openness and sustainability.

Yet trade volumes remain limited. There is no structured economic framework and political dialogue is modest. The potential is largely untapped. Morocco can become New Zealand's gateway to Africa. New Zealand currently has only three, uh, uh, embassies on the continent, Ethiopia, South Africa, and Egypt.

Opening a presence in Morocco would give New Zealand strategic reach across North and West Africa. What are the priority, uh, areas for cooperation can include sustainable agriculture and agritech water management and resilience, renewable energy, green hydrogen education, research, and innovation.

Morocco also offers platforms such as the siam, the International Agricultural Fair, and Halles, which connect global partners to African markets, particularly in AgriFood and Fisheries. Singapore offers an instructive comparison with a population quite similar to New Zealand.

It engages far more with Morocco leveraging its it as a gateway to Africa in digital systems capacity building, agri trade, and sustainable technologies. The Sahara issue is a sweet, generous case, unique in nature and context. Historically, the Sahara Tribes pledge allegiance to the Moroccan Sultan.

The green March of 1975 peacefully reaffirmed this reality. The Moroccan Sahara Bear bears no comparison with other territorial situations such as talal. It concerns Morocco sovereignty and territorial integrity. On October 31st, 2025, the UN Security Council adopted resolution 27 97 renewing mineo and reaffirming the autonomy plan presented by Morocco in 2007 as the basis for a political solution under Moroccan sovereignty.

The referendum option is obsolete since 25 years. Uh, it has not been mentioned at the UN General Assembly for the past 25 years. The cons, the Security Council consistently calls for a realistic, pragmatic, and durable solution based on compromise.

More than 120 countries now support the Moroccan autonomy plan. 24 of the 27 EU countries, over two thirds of the African countries, all the Gulf states, the majority of the Latin and the Caribbean countries, three permanent members of the Security Council, the United States, France, and the uk.

Beyond the political, political dimension, this dispute also has a growing security dimension. The prio operates in a highly fragile, salient environment marked by terrorism, trafficking and organized crime. There is increasing concern about the intersection between separatist, militancy and extremist networks in the region.

Even more troubling are documented practices in the T do camps, including the militarization of children, ideological indoctrination, and restrictions In freedom of movement. These practices are incompatible with international humanitarian standards and the convention on the rights of the child.

In contrast, the Moroccan Sahara is experiencing tangible development, high electoral participation, full integration, international institution, major infrastructure and social development, and nearly 30 foreign consulates opened in the region.

We have prepared a detailed fact sheet for you, which you have here. And as represented representatives of a principled and pragmatic country, we respectfully invite New Zealand to reassess its position in light of international momentum, regional security realities and its own law-based diplomacy, a balanced position such as that of the United Kingdom would consolidate New Zealand's credibility within the Commonwealth support un efforts for peaceful conflict resolution, contribute to stability in a region directly affected by terrorism,

and encourage closer cooperation with Morocco. This is not only a territorial issue, it's also a about regional stability, human dignity, and preventing new fault lines in an already fragile Sahel environment. Morocco and New Zealand share a commitment to international law and pragmatic multilateralism.

Morocco offers stability, vision, and responsibility in a complex region. New Zealand embodies in principled and forward-looking depl diplomacy in Indo-Pacific. Together we can bring, build a bridge between Africa, the Indo-Pacific, creating a platform for South south cooperation and our strength, our complimentary, our values converge, and the the time has come to elevate our relationship.

Thank you. Excellent. Thank you very much, ambassador. Appreciate your insights into, uh, Morocco and also the New Zealand Moroccan relationship. Great initiative. Yes. Uh, look, thank you very much, ambassador. And and welcome to the side Tasman. Thank you. We're an agricultural country. Yeah. Um, we export a lot of the, the red fruits also from all over. Uh, the UK was the first, uh, Morocco was the first country to sign a trade agreement with the UK post Brexit. And our trade has been going up, uh, I mean consistently.

And that's why I believe that a country like New Zealand, I understand the, the, for budget constraints, uh, I have brought that up about opening an embassy, but I think it could in the near future be, uh, something that you would really need to think about. Seriously.

Also, our king is the chair of the, uh, Jerusalem committee. So we have, Morocco has always played a, an, an important role in, uh, in the, the Israeli-Palestinian issue. On the International Treaty examination of the agreement to establish the Pacific Resilience Facility. Uh, we have three submitters we'll be hearing from today. The first is Council for International Development. We have them present. Excellent. Please come up to the table. Uh,

so we have received your, uh, submission and you have 10 minutes set down for the hearing today. Good Morning.

Um, thank you very much, Mr. Chair, that committee members that, um, my name is Todd Cle. I'm the Executive Director of the Council for International Development, um, and I'm joined by Mr. Sam London, our standards and humanitarian manager. Um, I'm pleased to joining to discuss that the Pacific Resilience Fund, uh, facility and to place it within the broader context of New Zealand's development engagement.

Uh, our core message today is twofold, strong support for the facility, but also the importance of New Zealand's contribution, not be, um, made through the existing ODA envelope. Enhancing climate to resilience across Pacifica is vital to wider regional stability and to New Zealand's border, uh, international humanitarian goals.

However, funding it within the facility will simply involve shifting pressures around the system and comes at a time of greater concerns from wider ODA cuts. Uh, the Council for International Development is New Zealand's peak body for international, uh, development and humanitarian NGOs.

Our membership spans, um, large well-known, uh, organizations as well as small specialists NGOs that often work very, very closely with partners across Pacific Asia, Africa, and beyond. Um, we also work closely with the Pacific Islands Association of NGOs known as Pang o um, SIDS counterpart in the Pacific, which represents 13 civil society peak bodies, all with their own diverse membership.

It was actually with, um, when in partnership with Pang that we made our written submission. Um, since membership is diverse, but what binds 'em is a commitment to principled and effective, uh, development and a long history of the coalface of development, um, initiatives. Everything from health, education, economic development, as well as, um, mitigating and responding to natural disasters and climate change.

Uh, the work is grounded in deep trusted relationships that have been built over time with Pacific Civil Societies, churches, communities, uh, and local authorities. These relationships means mean that NGOs can, uh, respond quite flexibly and quickly, um, in times of trouble.

And also that, that their work is locally informed and grounded in the Pacific, like elsewhere. Resilience not is not only built through infrastructure and systems, but through people connections, organizations and social networks. NGOs are often the bridge between the high level initiatives and then the on the ground realities in villages, communities, and outer islands.

Our members also complement and strengthen the New Zealand government's efforts, um, and the Pacific through the delivery of ODA, um, funded initiatives. This funding plus significant member, um, sourced funding supports a very broad scope of work from access to clean water, food security, disease prevention, skills development.

It's about schools, hospitals, villages, and businesses. Um, they're also respond often the responding very, very quickly in times of disaster. And many of our organizations, sorry, a number of our organizations are central, uh, to the pre-positioning of critical supplies across the Pacific. Also for New Zealand and Pacific Country, with deep social connections across the region, our prosperity and security is premised on having a safe and stable Pacific.

For all these reasons, the Council for International Development strongly supports the establishment of the Pacific Resilience Facility and New Zealand's early and constructive participation. We see the facilities a timely initiative that aligns closely with Pacific priorities, particularly on ownership and community led approaches. We see this not only as a climate mechanism, climate finance mechanism, we see it as a cost effective investment in the regional stability.

We particularly welcome emphasis on Pacific leadership governance and localization for this to see, um, succeed in practice. Pacific organizations must have realistic access to funding with proportionate accountability and safeguarding systems that enable rather than, uh, obstruct delivery for our own members, many of whom are already, um, significantly involved in resilience initiatives.

They have a crucial role to play as well. It's key that they're able to partner with Pacific organizations through the facility, uh, to help build capability and support long-term sustainability. Uh, but at this time it's key to register to, particularly to this select committee that the global Fi humanitarian development sector is going through a period of significant stress.

Um, we're seeing major donors that, particularly the United States, but also United Kingdom and France and many others, um, significantly reduce the ODA spend and reprioritize funding around the globe. We expect the, the impacts of this to be more evident in the Pacific as a number of existing programs, um, completed. So in the shrinking global aid environment, Pacific countries will face increasing competition for diminishing resources precisely at time where climate, health, economic pressures across the region are intensifying.

As a sector, we strongly encourage the government to ensure that New Zealand's contribution is funded through new and additional resources. Reallocating merely shifts the pressure around the system in practical terms of funding within the existing envelope, slowing the number of health initiatives, disaster preparedness programs, et cetera, are being delivered through other mechanisms.

This matters not only from a development perspective, but also from a national interest. One, as other donors retreat and aid flows, tighten, uh, strategic vacuums may emerge. The Pacific is facing, escalated and interconnected challenges from natural disasters, economic stress and health, um, and security such as the very, very deeply troubling increase in HIV alongside the, alongside the growing reach of, uh, transnational crime.

This should be a concern to New Zealand, to regional partners, and to you. In closing, the Pacific Resilience Facility is positive and significant initiative that presents a real opportunity to strengthen Pacific resilience. But the decisions around ODA levels in the Pacific now need to be made with, um, eyes open, open to the shifting con, um, context of global ODA cuts that will start to impact on the Pacific region.
